content:
# Numbers Chapter 20 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 172-173 | Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0127-0128 (LXX)
**Source Images:** `NUM_020_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_020_page_002.jpg`; `NUM_020_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_020_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Death of Miriam (v. 1)

**1** And the Sons of Israel, the whole congregation, came into the wilderness of Zin in the first month, and the people stayed in Kadesh. And Miriam died there and was buried there.

---

### The Waters of Meribah (vv. 2-13)

**2** And there was no water for the congregation, and they assembled themselves against Moses and against Aaron.

**3** And the people contended with Moses and spoke, saying, "Would that we had perished when our brothers perished before YHWH!

**4** And why have you brought the assembly of YHWH into this wilderness, to die there, we and our livestock?

**5** And why have you brought us up from Egypt, to bring us to this evil place? It is not a place of seed, or of figs, or of vines, or of pomegranates; and there is no water to drink."

**6** And Moses and Aaron went from the presence of the assembly to the entrance of the tent of meeting, and they fell upon their faces. And the glory of YHWH appeared to them.

**7** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**8** "Take the staff, and assemble the congregation, you and Aaron your brother, and speak to the rock before their eyes, and it shall give its water. And you shall bring forth for them water from the rock, and you shall give drink to the congregation and their livestock."

**9** And Moses took the staff from before YHWH, as he had commanded him.

**10** And Moses and Aaron assembled the assembly before the rock. And he said to them, "Hear now, you rebels! Shall we bring forth water for you from this rock?"

**11** And Moses raised his hand and struck the rock with his staff twice. And water came out abundantly, and the congregation drank, and their livestock.

**12** And YHWH said to Moses and to Aaron, "Because you did not believe in me, to sanctify me in the eyes of the Sons of Israel, therefore you shall not bring this assembly into the land which I have given to them."

**13** These are the waters of Meribah, where the Sons of Israel contended with YHWH, and he was sanctified among them.

---

### Edom Refuses Passage (vv. 14-21)

**14** And Moses sent messengers from Kadesh to the king of Edom: "Thus says your brother Israel: You know all the hardship that has found us,

**15** that our fathers went down to Egypt, and we dwelt in Egypt many days, and the Egyptians did evil to us and to our fathers.

**16** And we cried out to YHWH, and he heard our voice, and he sent a messenger and brought us out from Egypt. And behold, we are in Kadesh, a city at the edge of your border.

**17** Let us pass, please, through your land. We will not pass through field or through vineyard, and we will not drink water of a well. By the King's Highway we will go; we will not turn aside to the right or to the left, until we have passed through your border."

**18** And Edom said to him, "You shall not pass through me, lest I come out with the sword to meet you."

**19** And the Sons of Israel said to him, "By the highway we will go up, and if we drink of your water, I and my livestock, then I will pay its price. Only — it is nothing — let me pass through on my feet."

**20** And he said, "You shall not pass through." And Edom came out to meet him with a heavy people and with a strong hand.

**21** And Edom refused to give Israel passage through his border. And Israel turned away from him.

---

### The Death of Aaron (vv. 22-29)

**22** And they set out from Kadesh, and the Sons of Israel, the whole congregation, came to Mount Hor.

**23** And YHWH said to Moses and to Aaron at Mount Hor, on the border of the land of Edom, saying,

**24** "Aaron shall be gathered to his people, for he shall not enter the land which I have given to the Sons of Israel, because you rebelled against my mouth at the waters of Meribah.

**25** Take Aaron and Eleazar his son, and bring them up to Mount Hor.

**26** And strip Aaron of his garments, and put them on Eleazar his son. And Aaron shall be gathered and shall die there."

**27** And Moses did as YHWH commanded, and they went up to Mount Hor in the sight of all the congregation.

**28** And Moses stripped Aaron of his garments, and put them on Eleazar his son. And Aaron died there on the top of the mountain. And Moses and Eleazar came down from the mountain.

**29** And all the congregation saw that Aaron had perished, and they wept for Aaron thirty days, all the house of Israel.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes

1. **Verse 1 -- "in the first month" (בַּחֹדֶשׁ הָרִאשׁוֹן):** The Leningrad Codex reads bachodesh harishon without specifying the year. This is a significant chronological lacuna. Given that Miriam's death and Aaron's death (v. 28, later dated in 33:38 to the fifth month of the fortieth year) occur in the same chapter, the "first month" most likely refers to the fortieth year of the wilderness wandering, though the text is silent. The LXX reads εν τω μηνι τω πρωτω, equally without specifying the year. The thirty-eight years between Numbers 19 and Numbers 20 pass in narrative silence -- one of the most striking compressions in the Pentateuch.

2. **Verse 1 -- "And Miriam died there and was buried there" (וַתָּמָת שָׁם מִרְיָם וַתִּקָּבֵר שָׁם):** The death of Miriam is reported with extreme brevity -- no mourning period is mentioned (contrast Aaron's thirty days in v. 29 and Moses' thirty days in Deuteronomy 34:8). The LXX renders και ετελευτησεν εκει Μαριαμ και εταφη εκει. The juxtaposition of Miriam's death (v. 1) with the water crisis (v. 2) led rabbinic tradition (b. Ta'anit 9a) to connect Miriam with a miraculous well that ceased at her death. The formal rendering preserves the MT's starkness without supplying the mourning notice the text withholds.

3. **Verse 3 -- "Would that we had perished" (וְלוּ גָוַעְנוּ):** The particle lu introduces an irreal wish (counterfactual optative). The verb gava' (to expire, perish) is distinct from the common mut (to die); it carries connotations of breathing one's last. The phrase "when our brothers perished before YHWH" (בִּגְוַע אַחֵינוּ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה) refers to the deaths during Korah's rebellion (Numbers 16:32-35) and/or the subsequent plague (Numbers 17:14 [English 16:49]). The LXX renders οφελον απεθανομεν (would that we had died). The people's wish to have died in a previous divine judgment is a characteristic irony of the wilderness complaint tradition.

4. **Verse 5 -- "this evil place" (הַמָּקוֹם הָרָע הַזֶּה):** The adjective ra' (evil, bad) characterizes the location as inhospitable. The catalogue of what the place lacks -- seed (grain crops), figs, vines, pomegranates, and water -- constitutes a precise anti-catalogue of the promised land's bounty (cf. Deuteronomy 8:8). The LXX renders τον τοπον τον πονηρον τουτον (this wretched place). The rhetorical force depends on the contrast: where the promised land flows with abundance, the wilderness offers nothing.

5. **Verse 8 -- "speak to the rock" (וְדִבַּרְתֶּם אֶל־הַסֶּלַע):** The verb is second person plural (you shall speak), addressed to both Moses and Aaron. The noun sela' (cliff, crag) differs from the tsur (rock) of the Exodus 17:6 episode at Rephidim. There, Moses was commanded to strike (hikkita) the rock; here, he is commanded only to speak (dibbarta). The shift from striking to speaking is central to the theology of the passage: the means of provision has changed, but Moses reverts to the earlier method. The LXX reads λαλησατε προς την πετραν (speak to the rock), using the plural imperative. The formal rendering preserves both the plural address and the distinction between "speak" (command) and "strike" (action).

6. **Verse 10 -- "Hear now, you rebels" (שִׁמְעוּ־נָא הַמֹּרִים):** The term morim (rebels) derives from the root מרה (to be rebellious, contentious). Moses addresses the congregation with an epithet that, while arguably accurate, may constitute the presumption for which he is judged. The interrogative "Shall we bring forth water for you from this rock?" (הֲמִן־הַסֶּלַע הַזֶּה נוֹצִיא לָכֶם מָיִם) uses the first person plural, possibly attributing the miracle to Moses and Aaron rather than to YHWH alone. The LXX renders ακουσατε μοι οι απειθεις (hear me, you disobedient ones), using απειθεις rather than a form of the Hebrew morim. The nature of Moses' sin has been debated since antiquity: striking instead of speaking, striking twice, the angry address, the first-person plural claim, or some combination.

7. **Verse 11 -- "struck the rock with his staff twice" (וַיַּךְ אֶת־הַסֶּלַע בְּמַטֵּהוּ פַּעֲמָיִם):** The verb hikkah (he struck) with pa'amayim (twice) emphasizes the deviation from the divine command, which was to speak. The doubling may indicate frustration, doubt, or simple disobedience. Despite the disobedience, water came out "abundantly" (rabbim) -- the grace of provision is not withheld from the people even when the mediator fails. The LXX reads και επαταξεν την πετραν τη ραβδω δις (and he struck the rock with the staff twice), preserving the detail.

8. **Verse 12 -- "Because you did not believe in me" (יַעַן לֹא־הֶאֱמַנְתֶּם בִּי):** The verb he'emantem (Hiphil of אמן, to believe, trust) specifies the failure as one of faith. The phrase "to sanctify me" (לְהַקְדִּישֵׁנִי) adds the dimension of failed witness: Moses and Aaron did not demonstrate YHWH's holiness before the people. The punishment -- exclusion from the promised land -- is identical to the punishment of the entire exodus generation (Numbers 14:23), placing the leaders on the same footing as the faithless people. The LXX renders ουκ επιστευσατε μοι αγιασαι με (you did not believe me so as to sanctify me). The formal rendering preserves the causal construction (because ... therefore).

9. **Verse 13 -- "he was sanctified among them" (וַיִּקָּדֵשׁ בָּם):** The Niphal of קדש. Despite the failure of Moses and Aaron, YHWH was nonetheless sanctified -- the water flowed, the people drank, and divine holiness was demonstrated through judgment on the leaders themselves. The name "Meribah" (מְרִיבָה) derives from ריב (to contend, strive). The LXX renders ηγιασθη εν αυτοις (he was sanctified among them). The verse serves as an etiology for the place name.

10. **Verse 14 -- "Thus says your brother Israel" (כֹּה אָמַר אָחִיךָ יִשְׂרָאֵל):** The diplomatic address "your brother" invokes the kinship between Israel (Jacob) and Edom (Esau), recalling Genesis 25:19-34 and 32-33. The messenger formula "thus says" (koh amar) is typically prophetic or royal. The LXX renders ταδε λεγει ο αδελφος σου Ισραηλ. The appeal to brotherhood makes Edom's refusal in vv. 18-21 a violation of kinship obligation, anticipating the prophetic condemnations of Edom (Obadiah; Amos 1:11).

11. **Verse 17 -- "the King's Highway" (דֶּרֶךְ הַמֶּלֶךְ):** A well-known ancient route running north-south through Transjordan, from the Gulf of Aqaba through Edom, Moab, and Ammon to Damascus. The name may derive from its maintenance by royal authority or its status as a major interregional road. The LXX renders οδω βασιλικη (by the royal road). Archaeological evidence confirms the existence of this route as a major thoroughfare in the Late Bronze and Iron Ages.

12. **Verse 18 -- "lest I come out with the sword to meet you" (פֶּן־בַּחֶרֶב אֵצֵא לִקְרָאתֶךָ):** Edom's response is a direct military threat. The rejection of the kinship appeal is total. The LXX renders μηποτε εν μαχαιρα εξελθω εις συναντησιν σοι (lest I come out with the sword to meet you). Israel does not challenge this refusal, in accordance with Deuteronomy 2:4-6, where YHWH prohibits conflict with Edom because their land has been given to the descendants of Esau.

13. **Verse 19 -- "Only -- it is nothing -- let me pass through on my feet" (רַק אֵין דָּבָר בְּרַגְלַי אֶעֱבֹרָה):** The phrase rak ein davar is an asseverative minimization: "it is nothing, a trivial matter." The word beraglay (on my feet, i.e., on foot) emphasizes the harmless nature of the request -- mere pedestrian transit. The LXX renders αλλα το πραγμα ουδεν εστιν παρα το ορος παρελευσομαι (but the matter is nothing; by the mountain I will pass), reading the request somewhat differently. The formal rendering follows the MT.

14. **Verse 20 -- "with a heavy people and with a strong hand" (בְּעַם כָּבֵד וּבְיָד חֲזָקָה):** The phrase yad chazaqah (a strong hand) is characteristically used of YHWH's power in the exodus (Exodus 6:1; 13:9; Deuteronomy 4:34). Its application to Edom is deeply ironic: the nation that was delivered by YHWH's strong hand is now confronted by a human "strong hand" that blocks its way. The LXX renders εν οχλω βαρει και εν χειρι ισχυρα (with a heavy crowd and with a strong hand). The adjective kaved (heavy, numerous) echoes the "heavy" hardness of Pharaoh's heart (Exodus 7:14; 8:15; 9:7).

15. **Verse 22 -- "Mount Hor" (הֹר הָהָר):** Literally "Hor the mountain" or "the mountain of the mountain." The doubled form is unusual; it may be a proper name with an appositional common noun, or it may reflect an etymology where "Hor" itself means "mountain" in a non-Hebrew language, producing a bilingual tautology. The LXX renders Ωρ το ορος (Hor the mountain). The traditional identification is Jebel Nebi Harun, near Petra in modern Jordan, though this identification is not certain.

16. **Verse 24 -- "Aaron shall be gathered to his people" (יֵאָסֵף אַהֲרֹן אֶל־עַמָּיו):** The idiom "gathered to his people" (ne'esaf el-ammav) denotes death as a reunion with ancestors. It is distinct from burial (cf. Genesis 25:8; 35:29; 49:33). The Niphal of אסף carries the passive sense of being received or collected. The LXX renders προστεθητω Ααρων προς τον λαον αυτου (let Aaron be added to his people). The stated reason -- "because you rebelled against my mouth" (מְרִיתֶם אֶת־פִּי) -- uses the same root מרה from which "Meribah" may be associated, binding Aaron's fate to the water incident.

17. **Verse 26 -- "strip Aaron of his garments" (הַפְשֵׁט אֶת־אַהֲרֹן אֶת־בְּגָדָיו):** The Hiphil of פשׁט (to strip off). The garments (begadav) are the priestly vestments described in Exodus 28-29. The transfer of garments from Aaron to Eleazar effects the succession of the high priesthood in a single ritual act: investiture and divestiture occur simultaneously. The LXX renders εκδυσον Ααρων τα ιματια αυτου (strip Aaron of his garments). The stripping of a dying priest and re-clothing of his successor on a mountaintop, witnessed by Moses alone (besides Eleazar), is one of the most solemn scenes in the Pentateuch.

18. **Verse 28 -- "And Aaron died there on the top of the mountain" (וַיָּמָת אַהֲרֹן שָׁם בְּרֹאשׁ הָהָר):** Numbers 33:38-39 specifies the date as the first day of the fifth month of the fortieth year, and Aaron's age as one hundred and twenty-three years. The LXX reads και απεθανεν Ααρων εκει επι της κορυφης του ορους. The simplicity of the death notice -- no final speech, no elaborate blessing -- contrasts with the elaborate ritual that precedes it. Aaron dies already divested of his office; his death is private, witnessed only by his son and brother.

19. **Verse 29 -- "all the congregation saw that Aaron had perished" (וַיִּרְאוּ כָּל־הָעֵדָה כִּי גָוַע אַהֲרֹן):** The verb gava' (to expire) is the same verb the people used in their complaint in verse 3 -- "would that we had expired" (lu gava'nu). This lexical echo binds the chapter's beginning and end: the people wished for death, and death comes -- but to their high priest, not to them. The LXX renders και ειδεν πασα η συναγωγη οτι απελυθη Ααρων (and all the congregation saw that Aaron had departed), using a softer euphemism. The thirty-day mourning period matches that later observed for Moses (Deuteronomy 34:8) and is the maximum period of formal mourning in the biblical tradition.

### Structural Observations

- Numbers 20 is framed by two deaths: Miriam at the beginning (v. 1) and Aaron at the end (vv. 22-29). The chapter thus narrates the loss of two of Israel's three original leaders within a single literary unit. Moses, the surviving leader, is himself sentenced to die outside the promised land (v. 12). The entire generation of exodus leadership is condemned in this chapter.

- The water crisis at Meribah (vv. 2-13) stands as the narrative center and theological pivot. It echoes the earlier water-from-rock episode at Rephidim (Exodus 17:1-7), creating a deliberate parallel between the beginning and end of the wilderness period. The key differences -- speaking versus striking, sela' versus tsur, singular versus double strike -- highlight that the passage is not mere repetition but a distinct event with distinct consequences.

- The Edom episode (vv. 14-21) introduces the geopolitical obstacles of the Transjordanian approach to the promised land. The diplomatic language -- the messenger formula, the historical summary, the kinship appeal, the promise of compensation -- follows ancient Near Eastern conventions for transit negotiations. Edom's refusal forces a detour that leads to Mount Hor, which leads to Aaron's death, creating a chain of consequence: Edom's rejection becomes the occasion for the priestly succession.

- The chapter exhibits a pattern of threes: three leaders (Miriam, Aaron, Moses -- all judged or dead), three confrontations (people versus Moses, Moses versus the rock, Israel versus Edom), and three divine speeches (vv. 7-8, v. 12, vv. 23-26). This triadic structure underscores the comprehensive nature of the transition occurring in this chapter.

- The garment transfer on Mount Hor (vv. 25-28) is the only priestly succession described in the Pentateuch. Unlike royal succession, which often involves conflict (cf. the Davidic succession narrative), the Aaronic succession is mandated by YHWH, performed by Moses, and executed without resistance. Aaron's silent compliance -- he speaks no words in the entire chapter -- is its own kind of eloquence: the high priest who once made the golden calf (Exodus 32) now obediently yields his vestments and his life on a mountain, in an act of priestly submission that mirrors Abraham's obedience on Mount Moriah.
